metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite a concrete profiling result / measured bottleneck that justifies overriding the 'intentionally no batched-commit fast path' stance in `docs/scale-envelope.md`, or update that document to record the deliberate trade-off and the evidence driving it.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a batched-commit fast path that docs/scale-envelope.md explicitly documents as intentionally absent for durability reasons, without citing any profiling evidence, scale estimate, or AC that overrides that standing design decision — a premature-optimization signal.
findings.location: ## Why: 'The single-event write path … commits one git graph per event (~3.3 commits/event)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Why now / evidence' sub-section to the plan citing a profiling run (e.g. 'import of N tickets took Xs; batch commit reduces it to Ys'), and update docs/scale-envelope.md to reflect the new design decision and its rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the test coverage bullet to name all four rollback scenarios explicitly: validation-failure rollback, rename-failure rollback, `git add`-failure rollback, and non-recoverable `git commit`-failure rollback.
findings.finding: The test coverage bullet 'validation/commit rollback' in `test_store_batch_write.py` is condensed but the AC enumerates four distinct failure points (validation, rename, `git add`, non-recoverable `git commit`); an executor may write only two cases and believe the AC is satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — new unit tests bullet and all-or-nothing rollback bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'validation/commit rollback' in the test coverage list with: 'rollback on validation failure, rollback on rename failure (partial batch), rollback on git-add failure, rollback on non-recoverable git-commit failure'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least two named test cases for `batch_write_and_push` in `test_store_batch_write.py`: one for push-succeeds (commit is durable before push attempt) and one for push-fails (exception is swallowed / best-effort semantics hold and committed events are not rolled back).
findings.finding: `batch_write_and_push` is a newly introduced caller-facing function whose test coverage is entirely absent from the AC's named test list — the enumerated cases in `test_store_batch_write.py` cover only `batch_stage_and_commit` scenarios. In particular, the push-failure (best-effort swallow) and push-success paths are never named, so the suite can go green while the push half of the batch variant is broken.
findings.location: AC line: `batch_write_and_push` variant (AC bullet 2) vs. test enumeration (AC bullet 7: `test_store_batch_write.py` case list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC bullet 7 test list: 'batch_write_and_push push-succeeds (events committed before push), batch_write_and_push push-fails (best-effort: commit retained, push error swallowed)'. These can be implemented with a mock git remote that raises on push, asserting the function returns the event count and does not re-raise.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a commit message specification for `batch_stage_and_commit` — e.g., `ticket: batch {N} events` for a homogeneous batch or a multi-line body listing each `{event_type} {ticket_id}` — so implementers and downstream git-log consumers have a stable contract.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the commit message format for a batch commit. The single-event path uses `f'ticket: {event_type} {ticket_id}'`, which is singular. A batch spans multiple `(ticket_id, event_dict)` pairs with potentially different ticket IDs and event types, so the implementer will invent a message without guidance — risking divergence from the bash-parity commit-message contract or producing an uninformative message that breaks downstream tools that parse commit messages.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 1: 'exactly one commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or AC: 'Commit message: `ticket: batch {N} events` for N>1; falls back to the single-event form `ticket: {event_type} {ticket_id}` when N=1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that pre-lock staging temp files (all N) are cleaned up via `finally: [_silent_unlink(t) for t in staging_temps]` — mirroring the single-event path's `finally: _silent_unlink(staging)` — to prevent temp-file accumulation on `RebaseGuard` / `LockTimeout` failures.
findings.finding: The rollback specification covers failures inside the lock (rename / git add / non-recoverable commit), but does not address cleanup of the N pre-lock staging temp files when a `RebaseGuard` or `LockTimeout` is raised before or during lock acquisition after some (or all) temps have been written. In the single-event path, the `finally: _silent_unlink(staging)` covers this. For the batch, the plan says nothing about how the N temp files are cleaned up on a pre-lock exception path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope rollback clause: 'Pre-lock staging temps are always cleaned up in a `finally` block regardless of failure cause (mirrors single-event path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe the backward-compat strategy for generalizing `_recover_from_unmerged` (e.g., add an optional `paths` parameter defaulting to the single file, so the single-event call site requires no change) to ensure AC6's 'single-event path unchanged' guarantee is mechanically enforced.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `_recover_from_unmerged` to 're-add the FULL batch path list on retry' (Scope/AC4) but does not describe how the existing single-event call site is kept compatible — e.g., whether the signature gains an optional parameter with a default, or whether the function is overloaded. Without this, the implementer must guess the generalization strategy, risking an inadvertent breakage of the single-event recovery path that AC6 promises to leave unchanged.
findings.location: Scope ('Generalize `_recover_from_unmerged` to re-add the FULL batch path list on retry') and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add: 'Generalize `_recover_from_unmerged(paths: list[Path])` by replacing the implicit single-path assumption with an explicit `paths` parameter; update the single-event call site to pass `[staged_path]` so behavior is identical to today.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the Scope section that the `stage_and_commit` call site for `_recover_from_unmerged` (line 161, `event_append.py`) must also be updated (e.g. passing `[relative_path]`) when the function signature is generalized to accept a path list.
findings.finding: `_recover_from_unmerged` is called from `stage_and_commit` with a single `str` argument (`relative_path`), but the plan says to 'Generalize `_recover_from_unmerged` to re-add the FULL batch path list on retry' — which implies a signature change (e.g. `str` → `Sequence[str]`). The plan does not enumerate the consequent update to the existing `stage_and_commit` call site (line 161 of `event_append.py`), which must also be adapted (e.g. wrapping the single path in a list) to remain compatible after the signature change.
findings.location: Scope section ('Generalize `_recover_from_unmerged`...') / AC line 4 ('re-adds the full batch path list') / `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` line 161
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Scope: 'The generalized `_recover_from_unmerged` accepts `event_relpaths: Sequence[str]`; the existing `stage_and_commit` call site passes `[relative_path]` (a 1-element list) so the single-event path remains behaviour-identical.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the return value of `batch_write_and_push` explicitly in its AC (e.g. 'returns the event count; push failure is logged and suppressed, mirroring the existing `write_and_push` contract').
findings.finding: `batch_write_and_push` return value is not stated anywhere in the plan; only `batch_stage_and_commit` is specified to 'return the event count'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `batch_write_and_push` AC: 'returns the event count (same as `batch_stage_and_commit`); push failure is logged and silently suppressed, matching the existing `write_and_push` best-effort contract'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a design-note (ADR entry, CLAUDE.md section, or docstring block at module top) capturing the batch-primitive contract: one lock acquire, one commit per batch, all-or-nothing rollback, per-event UUID files are the dedup unit — so future callers and maintainers have a persistent reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural primitive (batch commit under one lock, all-or-nothing rollback, per-event UUID file invariant) that B2 and future callers depend on, but specifies no update to any persistent design document (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc). The invariants are described only in the ticket itself.
findings.location: Scope section / Acceptance Criteria (no doc-update AC listed)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring block to `event_append.py` (or a short ADR) documenting: (1) the batch commit invariant (one lock, one commit), (2) the all-or-nothing rollback guarantee and what it covers, (3) that dedup/replay key off per-event UUID files not commit boundaries. Reference it from the AC as 'docstring / ADR updated'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retire/cleanup path for uuid files orphaned by a crash-during-rollback: either (a) specify that `batch_stage_and_commit` opens by scanning for and removing any unindexed `{ticket}/{ts}-{uuid}-{TYPE}.json` files not belonging to the current batch, or (b) document that the existing `_recover_from_unmerged` or a separate startup routine handles orphan detection, or (c) explicitly accept the risk and document why it is bounded.
findings.finding: The plan does not address cleanup of uuid-named files orphaned by a process crash that occurs after some renames succeed but before the rollback unlinks complete (or before the commit finishes). These files will persist across restarts, polluting the git working tree with unindexed, uncommitted event files.
findings.location: Scope section — all-or-nothing rollback; AC: rollback AC line

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the 'Why' section stating why the dedicated batch primitive is the right design (e.g., minimal scope confined to `event_append.py`, aligns with the existing rename+add+commit pattern, zero blast-radius on the lock module, directly testable in isolation).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section provides only a NEGATIVE rationale (the lock is not re-entrant, so callers cannot loop `stage_and_commit`), but omits a POSITIVE rationale explaining why a dedicated batch primitive — rather than any other design — is the right fit for this codebase. The absence of a positive rationale leaves the approach under-motivated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rename-to-existing behavior: either document that the rename is overwrite-safe (idempotent by content since uuid == same bytes) or add an explicit pre-rename existence check with a defined policy (skip, overwrite, or raise a deduplicated-batch error).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the atomic-rename step is idempotent when a uuid-named file already exists on disk from a previous partially-rolled-back run. If `os.rename` raises `FileExistsError` (non-overwrite) on a uuid that a prior failed rollback left behind, a legitimate retry of the same batch will fail incorrectly; if the rename silently overwrites, idempotency holds but this relies on unspecified OS behavior the plan should make explicit.
findings.location: Scope section (atomic-rename step) and AC: All-or-nothing rollback
findings.suggested_fix: Use `os replace()` (POSIX atomic overwrite) instead of `os.rename()` and document it: since same uuid ⟹ same canonical bytes, an overwrite is content-idempotent. Add an AC line: 'If a target uuid file already exists, `os.replace()` overwrites it (idempotent by content); this is safe because canonical bytes for a uuid are deterministic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements for `batch_stage_and_commit`: at minimum specify that validation failures, rollback completions, and commit success each emit a structured log line (with batch size and error details), so operators can detect and debug batch-path failures without instrumenting git history.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (structured log lines, metrics counters, or traces) for the new batch failure points: validation errors, rename failures, git-add/commit errors, and rollback completions. Operators have no signal to detect a consistently-failing batch path or to distinguish it from single-event failures.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — new unit tests section; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that at least two concrete, imminent consumers of `batch_stage_and_commit` are identified and named (with their stories/tickets) before implementing, or document why the single-consumer case justifies the complexity.
findings.finding: batch_stage_and_commit has zero existing call-sites; the plan names only one future consumer ('the importer, B2') which is a planned story — this is a premature abstraction that fails the Rule-of-Three bar.
findings.location: ## Why / ## Scope: 'A dedicated batch primitive is the seam bulk callers (the importer, B2) use'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `import_ndjson.py` (the named consumer) will adopt `batch_stage_and_commit` given that it currently calls high-level API functions (not `stage_and_commit` directly), and whether a companion story is needed to complete the wiring — or revise the stated consumer.
findings.finding: The named primary consumer (`import_ndjson.py`) does not call `stage_and_commit` at all today — it calls high-level API functions (`create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`, `comment`, etc.) that each go through `_seam.append_event` → `write_and_push`. Wiring it to `batch_stage_and_commit` would require changes well outside the stated scope of this plan, putting the single stated consumer out of reach without additional work.
findings.location: ## Why: 'A dedicated batch primitive is the seam bulk callers (the importer, B2) use to collapse N commits into 1'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
